schools.summary: He offers himself to temptation to be our mediator by example (Augustine); led willingly, by desire for the conflict (Jerome); the Spirit is the Holy Spirit (Gregory).
Calvin: two reasons — to come forth a new man after the fast, and to undergo trial; Henry/Lightfoot: the duel of the Seed of the woman and the serpent (Gen 3:15).
Wesley: after the clearest light and strongest consolation, expect the sharpest temptations.
A deliberate parallel with Moses (Ex 34:28) and Elijah (1 Kgs 19:8); he ate nothing (Lk 4:2); a fast of communion, not ceremony.
Wesley: the ensuing hunger prepared him for the first temptation.
The assault through gluttony, by which he conquered the first man (Gregory, Ambrose); Jerome's dilemma; Leo: Christ answers by Scripture, not miracle.
'If thou art Son of God' challenges the Father's word at the baptism; hunger weakens the body for the assault.
Calvin: a direct attack on Christ's faith, to drive him to unlawful means of getting food.

themes: If thou be the Son of God

schools.summary: From Deuteronomy 8:3 (the manna); the three answers all from Deuteronomy, words familiar from the phylacteries (Deut 6).
Calvin: men live by God's secret blessing; Christ trusts the Father to sustain him rather than work a self-serving miracle.
Wesley: thus we answer all Satan's suggestions — no need to work a miracle without the Father's intimation.

themes: man not live by bread alone

schools.summary: Satan lies in wait even in sacred places, transfiguring as an angel of light and citing Scripture irreverently (Remigius, Ambrose, Chrysostom).
The temple 'pinnacle' (perhaps the king's gallery) of dizzying height; Luke puts this temptation third (geographical order).
Calvin: the Evangelists did not aim at exact chronological order.

debates.question: Why do Matthew and Luke order the temptations differently?

sides.position: the Evangelists did not aim at exact chronology
different impressions left on the informants

schools.summary: A temptation to presumption, quoting Ps 91:11-12 perverted; the means used against one temptation turned to another (Clarke).
Calvin: Satan's malice in misapplying Scripture to make life deadly — a daily stratagem.
Christ quotes Deuteronomy 6:16, showing the devil misapplied the promise; he answers the false citation with a true one, not arguing the misuse.
Calvin: God's promised protection belongs to those who submit to his guidance — we cannot claim his promises while disobeying his commands.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the winnowing fan and does not bear on v.7.

themes: thou shalt not tempt the Lord
promises in the path of obedience

schools.summary: A mental display of worldly glory; an arrogant vaunt, for Satan cannot bestow all kingdoms (Origen, Jerome); the man tempted, the same God served by angels (Gregory).
A visionary, not bodily, panorama — no mountain surveys all kingdoms; all passed in the region of the spirit (Robertson, Ellicott).
Calvin: by voluntary permission, not weakness, Satan affected Christ's eyes with the vision.

themes: all the kingdoms of the world

schools.summary: Satan claims rule over all the world's kingdoms 'with plausibility,' since they are wicked (Luke 4:6 'delivered unto me'); the bid to gain them by idolatry.
Wesley: here Satan unmasks himself, and Christ now calls him by his own name.

themes: all these will I give thee

schools.summary: 'Begone, Satan' — a decided rebuke to the boldest, most impudent attack; answered from Deuteronomy 6:13 (the childhood lessons).
Calvin/Wesley: distinct from the word to Peter (16:23) — here he simply bids Satan go to his own place.
The devil leaves 'for a time' (Lk 4:13) to return; angels come and minister (repeated ministrations), bringing food.
Gregory/Hilary: the man tempted, the same God served by angels; their ministry will attend us too when we overcome.
Calvin: no truce till fully tried; God restrains Satan's power, and angels serve the victor (Wesley: to feed and congratulate).
A whole year of Judean ministry (John 1-4) falls in the gap the Synoptics skip; Jesus withdraws to Galilee on John's imprisonment.
Chrysostom: not to court peril but to withdraw — he retires to soften Jewish hostility; none harms a holy man unless God permits.
Calvin: the Synoptics pass over the interval because John's ministry still ran alongside Christ's.
Rejected at Nazareth (Lk 4:14-30), he settles at Capernaum (Tell Hum), a populous lakeside commercial town — his 'own city.'
Calvin/Clarke: he bade Nazareth a final farewell, utterly forsaking it because they rejected his word.
Matthew's 'that it might be fulfilled' formula introducing Isaiah 9:1-2; Nazareth fulfilled one prediction (2:23), Capernaum another.
Isaiah's geography — Zebulun, Naphtali, 'Galilee of the Gentiles' (mixed, half-heathen population); 'by the way of the sea' = near the sea.
Isaiah's words applied to the Messiah — the district in spiritual darkness/death sees the new era dawn when Christ comes; 'sat' (not 'walked') heightens the misery.
Wesley: a gradation — walked, then sat in darkness, then the shadow of death; the light is Christ himself.

themes: sat in darkness saw great light

schools.summary: Christ's Galilean preaching takes up John's message (3:2), after a months-long Judean ministry; 'began' = his stated public preaching.
He waits till John is imprisoned, lest the crowd be split (Chrysostom); preaches 'the kingdom of heaven,' not earthly goods (Remigius).
Clarke: every God-commissioned preacher begins with repentance, as Christ does here.

themes: from that time began to preach

schools.summary: He chose untaught fishermen, not philosophers, lest success be ascribed to learning (Augustine); called at their work, they followed at a single bidding (Chrysostom, Gregory).
Remember John 1:35-42 — they were already disciples, not called from nothing; the casting-net thrown over the shoulder.
Henry: as he began to preach, he began to gather the future hearers and preachers of his doctrine.
'Fishers of men' = gospel ministers winning souls (Lk 5:10); the call is the 'germ of a parable' (13:47), summoning already-disciples to leave their trade.
Clarke: a few solemn words that alarm the conscience — come after me, receive my doctrine, imitate me.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns Deuteronomy 8:3 and does not bear on v.19.

schools.summary: Immediate obedience — they left their nets (their livelihood, perhaps all their property) at once, evidence of sincerity.
Clarke: a change every way to their worldly disadvantage, proving their wholehearted devotion.
James and John mending/preparing their nets (katartizō); possibly the 'breaking' of Luke 5:6 an undesigned coincidence; called as the first pair.
They left more — ship, trade, and father (with the hired servants, Mk 1:20) — at Christ's call, the cost of discipleship (Lk 14:26).
Calvin: the power of Christ's voice — those he draws are inwardly addressed by his Spirit to obey.
As a king gathers an army before war, Christ collects his soldiers (Pseudo-Chrysostom); an example for teachers to be active and impartial (Remigius).
The first of three Galilean tours with the four fishermen; the 'gospel of the kingdom' implies vast unrecorded teaching.
Henry: an industrious preacher going about teaching in the synagogues.
The report spreads even to Gentile Syria; they bring every kind of sufferer — diseased, tormented, demon-possessed, lunatic, palsied — and he heals all.
Wesley: demoniacs, lunatics, and paralytics — the most deplorable and helpless cases — all healed.
Crowds and crowds from all Palestine including Decapolis (the ten cities east of Galilee); an outpouring unmatched by any cause.
Clarke: the verse joins to chapter 5 — these multitudes are the Sermon's audience; great crowds a proof of the Messiah's days.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the third temptation and does not bear on v.25.
